corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: Our focus is different-we train metrics that are not only state-of-the-art in conventional IID experimental setups, but also robust in the presence of scarce and out-of-distribution training data.
First, we benchmark BLEURT against existing text generation metrics on the last 3 years of the WMT Metrics Shared Task .
We test BLEURT's ability to adapt to different tasks with the WebNLG 2017 Challenge Dataset .
In this section, we evaluate BLEURT's performance on three tasks from a data-to-text dataset, the WebNLG Challenge 2017 .
Dataset and Evaluation Tasks: The WebNLG challenge benchmarks systems that produce natural language description of entities (e.g., buildings, cities, artists) from sets of 1 to 5 RDF triples.
6 6 Related Work The WMT shared metrics competition has inspired 6 Do those results imply that BLEU and ROUGE should be removed from future versions of BLEURT?
6 6 Related Work The WMT shared metrics competition has inspired 6 Do those results imply that BLEU and ROUGE should be removed from future versions of BLEURT?
On the other hand the removal may hurt future tasks in which BLEU or ROUGE actually correlate with human assessments.
On the other hand the removal may hurt future tasks in which BLEU or ROUGE actually correlate with human assessments.
BERTScore proposes replacing the hard n-gram overlap of BLEU with a soft-overlap using BERT embeddings.
To understand the relationship between pretraining time and downstream accuracy, we pretrain several versions of BLEURT and we fine-tune them on WMT17 data, varying the number of pretraining steps.
Most gains are obtained during the first 400,000 steps, that is, after about 2 epochs over our synthetic dataset.
The submissions are evaluated on 3 aspects: semantics, grammar, and fluency.
Oppositely, BLEU and ROUGE have a negative impact.
Oppositely, BLEU and ROUGE have a negative impact.
Yet, evaluation metrics have lagged behind, as the most popular choices (e.g., BLEU and ROUGE) may correlate poorly with human judgments.
Yet, evaluation metrics have lagged behind, as the most popular choices (e.g., BLEU and ROUGE) may correlate poorly with human judgments.
We propose BLEURT, a learned evaluation metric based on BERT that can model human judgments with a few thousand possibly biased training examples.
BLEURT provides state-ofthe-art results on the last three years of the WMT Metrics shared task and the WebNLG Competition dataset.
To illustrate, BLEU and ROUGE , two popular metrics, rely on N-gram overlap.
To illustrate, BLEU and ROUGE , two popular metrics, rely on N-gram overlap.
Because those metrics are only sensitive to lexical variation, they cannot appropriately reward semantic or syntactic variations of a given reference.
Thus, they have been repeatedly shown to correlate poorly with human judgment, in particular when all the systems to compare have a similar level of accuracy .
To illustrate, consider the WMT Metrics Shared Task, an annual benchmark in which translation metrics are compared on their ability to imitate human assessments.

And indeed, the IID assumption is particularly problematic in NLG evaluation because of domain drifts, that have been the main target of the metrics literature, but also because of quality drifts: NLG systems tend to get better over time, and therefore a model trained on ratings data from 2015 may fail to distinguish top performing systems in 2019, especially for newer research tasks.

downstream_contract.intended_use: Feed this JSON to a later local open-source model to write an overview.
downstream_contract.grounding_rule: The downstream generator should write from evidence_units.location.sentence and context_window, not from unsupported outside knowledge.
downstream_contract.score_warning: Scores are ranking and filtering signals, not calibrated probabilities.
